title_en: The Battles and the Conquest of Makkah
content_id:
Kampanye-kampanye militer Nabi, yang dikenal sebagai ghazawat ketika Nabi terlibat secara pribadi dan saraya ketika beliau mengirim pasukan di bawah seorang komandan, menempati bagian substansial dari bagian sirah al-Bidayah. Ibn Kathir menyajikan kampanye-kampanye ini bukan sebagai keingintahuan historis tetapi sebagai peristiwa-peristiwa yang dibimbing secara ilahi yang menetapkan kondisi-kondisi bagi penyebaran Islam di seluruh Jazirah Arab dan seterusnya. Perang Badar pada tahun kedua Hijriah diperlakukan sebagai peristiwa militer tunggal yang paling signifikan dari era Islam awal. Tiga ratus tiga belas orang beriman, yang kurang terlengkapi dan menghadapi kekuatan Quraisy sekitar seribu orang, meraih kemenangan yang menentukan. Ibn Kathir menyajikan ayat-ayat Al-Quran dari Surah al-Anfal yang diwahyukan sehubungan dengan Badar sebagai komentar ilahi atas pertempuran itu, menetapkan prinsip teologis bahwa kemenangan berasal dari Allah dan bahwa komunitas Muslim tidak boleh bergantung pada jumlah atau keunggulan material. Pembunuhan para pemimpin Quraisy di Badar disajikan sebagai titik balik yang menegakkan kredibilitas komunitas Muslim baru di hadapan suku-suku Arabia.

Perang Uhud pada tahun ketiga Hijriah disajikan dengan pelajaran menyakitkannya tentang konsekuensi ketidaktaatan terhadap perintah Nabi. Para pemanah yang ditempatkan di atas gunung meninggalkan posisi mereka dalam mengejar rampasan kemenangan sebelum pertempuran diputuskan, memungkinkan kavaleri Quraisy untuk mengepung pasukan Muslim. Tujuh puluh orang sahabat gugur, termasuk Hamzah ibn 'Abd al-Muthallib, paman Nabi dan salah satu pahlawan besar Islam awal. Nabi sendiri terluka, dan laporan palsu tentang kematiannya menyebabkan kekacauan sementara. Ibn Kathir menguraikan pelajaran-pelajaran Al-Quran dari Uhud secara ekstensif: bahwa ujian dan kemunduran adalah di antara sarana ilahi untuk menguji dan memurnikan orang-orang beriman, bahwa para syuhada Uhud mencapai kedudukan tertinggi, dan bahwa kemunduran sementara komunitas di Uhud adalah konsekuensi dari tindakan ketidaktaatan tertentu daripada bukti bahwa orang-orang Muslim ditinggalkan. Perang Khandaq (Parit) pada tahun kelima menyaksikan komunitas Madinah bertahan dari pengepungan koalisi melalui inovasi strategis menggali parit, dan akhirnya koalisi itu dibubarkan oleh angin ilahi dan perselisihan internal.

Perjanjian Hudaibiyyah pada tahun keenam merepresentasikan salah satu peristiwa yang paling instruktif secara teologis dari periode Madinah. Umat Islam berangkat ke Makkah untuk melaksanakan Umrah dan diblokir oleh Quraisy, yang mengarah pada negosiasi yang menghasilkan perjanjian yang tampak merugikan permukaannya: umat Islam yang melarikan diri dari Makkah ke Madinah akan dikembalikan, tetapi umat Islam yang melarikan diri dari Madinah ke Makkah tidak akan dikembalikan; umat Islam akan mundur pada tahun itu tanpa melaksanakan Umrah. Banyak sahabat termasuk 'Umar ibn al-Khattab merasa terganggu oleh syarat-syaratnya, namun Nabi menerimanya, dan Allah mewahyukan bahwa itu adalah 'kemenangan yang nyata.' Ibn Kathir menyajikan materi Al-Quran dan hadis tentang peristiwa ini sebagai menunjukkan wawasan kenabian dan kebijaksanaan strategis perjanjian yang akhirnya, yang mengantarkan pada periode damai di mana Islam menyebar secara luas. Pelanggaran perjanjian berikutnya oleh sekutu-sekutu Quraisy memberikan kesempatan untuk Penaklukan Makkah pada tahun kedelapan Hijriah, ketika Nabi berbaris menuju Makkah dengan pasukan sepuluh ribu orang dan kota itu diambil hampir tanpa pertumpahan darah.

Penaklukan Makkah disajikan oleh Ibn Kathir sebagai pemenuhan janji ilahi yang diulang berkali-kali dalam Al-Quran dan sebagai salah satu momen transformatif terbesar dalam sejarah manusia. Nabi memasuki Makkah dengan rendah hati, kepalanya menunduk dalam rasa syukur kepada Allah, dan menyatakan amnesti umum bagi penduduk Makkah yang telah menganiaya umat Islam selama dua puluh tahun. Berhala-berhala di dalam dan sekitar Ka'bah, berjumlah tiga ratus enam puluh menurut berbagai riwayat, dihancurkan, dan Nabi menyatakan 'Telah datang kebenaran dan telah lenyap kebatilan, sesungguhnya kebatilan itu selalu lenyap.' Konversi kaum Quraisy, termasuk mantan musuh-musuh seperti Abu Sufyan dan Khalid ibn al-Walid yang menjadi salah satu pejuang terbesar Islam, didokumentasikan. Perang Hunain dan pengepungan Ta'if menyusul tak lama kemudian, dan tahun terakhir kehidupan Nabi menyaksikan delegasi-delegasi dari seluruh Arabia datang untuk menerima Islam, peristiwa yang dirujuk dalam Surah al-Nashr sebagai datangnya kemenangan Allah dan pembukaan, menandakan penyelesaian misi kenabian di Jazirah Arabia.
content_en:
The military campaigns of the Prophet, known as ghazawat when the Prophet participated personally and saraya when he dispatched forces under a commander, occupy a substantial portion of the Bidayah's seerah section. Ibn Kathir presents these campaigns not as historical curiosities but as divinely guided events that established the conditions for the spread of Islam across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. The battle of Badr in the second year of Hijra is treated as the most significant single military event of the early Islamic era. Three hundred and thirteen believers, poorly equipped and facing a Qurayshi force of approximately one thousand, achieved a decisive victory. Ibn Kathir presents the Quranic verses of Surah al-Anfal that were revealed in connection with Badr as divine commentary on the battle, establishing the theological principle that victory is from Allah and that the Muslim community should not rely on numbers or material superiority. The killing of the Qurayshi leaders at Badr is presented as a turning point that established the credibility of the new Muslim community before the tribes of Arabia.

The battle of Uhud in the third year of Hijra is presented with its painful lesson of the consequences of disobeying the Prophet's command. The archers stationed on the mountain withdrew from their position in pursuit of the spoils of victory before the battle was decided, allowing the Qurayshi cavalry to encircle the Muslim force. Seventy companions were killed, including Hamzah ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle and one of the great heroes of early Islam. The Prophet himself was injured, and the false report of his death caused temporary disarray. Ibn Kathir draws out the Quranic lessons from Uhud extensively: that trials and setbacks are among the divine means of testing and purifying the believers, that the martyrs of Uhud achieved the highest station, and that the community's temporary reversal at Uhud was a consequence of a specific act of disobedience rather than evidence that the Muslims were abandoned. The battle of Khandaq (the Trench) in the fifth year saw the Medinan community survive a coalition siege through the strategic innovation of digging a trench, and the eventual dispersal of the coalition by a divine wind and internal disputes.

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in the sixth year represents one of the most theologically instructive events of the Medinan period. The Muslims set out for Makkah to perform 'Umrah and were blocked by the Quraysh, leading to negotiations that produced a treaty that appeared disadvantageous on its surface: Muslims who fled Makkah to Madinah would be returned, but Muslims who fled Madinah to Makkah would not be returned; the Muslims would withdraw for that year without performing 'Umrah. Many companions including 'Umar ibn al-Khattab were distressed by the terms, but the Prophet accepted them, and Allah revealed that it was 'a clear victory.' Ibn Kathir presents the Quranic and hadith material on this event as demonstrating the prophetic insight and the ultimate strategic wisdom of the treaty, which led to a period of peace during which Islam spread extensively. The subsequent breaking of the treaty by the Qurayshi allies provided the occasion for the Conquest of Makkah in the eighth year of Hijra, when the Prophet marched on Makkah with an army of ten thousand and the city was taken almost without bloodshed.

The Conquest of Makkah is presented by Ibn Kathir as the fulfillment of the divine promise made repeatedly in the Quran and as one of the great transformative moments of human history. The Prophet entered Makkah humbly, his head bowed in gratitude to Allah, and declared a general amnesty for the people of Makkah who had persecuted the Muslims for twenty years. The idols in and around the Ka'bah, numbering three hundred and sixty according to various narrations, were destroyed, and the Prophet declared 'Truth has come and falsehood has departed, indeed falsehood is ever departing.' The conversion of the Quraysh, including former enemies such as Abu Sufyan and Khalid ibn al-Walid who became among the greatest warriors of Islam, is documented. The battles of Hunayn and the siege of Ta'if followed closely, and the final year of the Prophet's life saw delegations from across Arabia coming to accept Islam, the event referred to in Surah al-Nasr as the coming of Allah's victory and the opening, signaling the completion of the prophetic mission on the Arabian Peninsula.

The military campaigns of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم, known as ghazawat when النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم participated personally and saraya when he dispatched forces under a commander, occupy a substantial portion of the Bidayah's seerah section. Ibn Kathir presents these campaigns not as historical curiosities but as divinely guided events that established the conditions for the spread of Islam across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. Ibn Kathir presents the Quranic verses of Surah al-Anfal that were revealed in connection with Badr as divine commentary on the battle, establishing the theological principle that victory is from الله and that the Muslim community should not rely on numbers or material superiority. The killing of the Qurayshi leaders at Badr is presented as a turning point that established the credibility of the new Muslim community before the tribes of Arabia.

The battle of Uhud in the third year of Hijra is presented with its painful lesson of the consequences of disobeying النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's command. The archers stationed on the mountain withdrew from their position in pursuit of the spoils of victory before the battle was decided, allowing the Qurayshi cavalry to encircle the Muslim force. The Prophet himself was injured, and the false report of his death caused temporary disarray. Ibn Kathir draws out the Quranic lessons from Uhud extensively: that trials and setbacks are among the divine means of testing and purifying the believers, that the martyrs of Uhud achieved the highest station, and that the community's temporary reversal at Uhud was a consequence of a specific act of disobedience rather than evidence that the Muslims were abandoned. The battle of Khandaq (the Trench) in the fifth year saw the Medinan community survive a coalition siege through the strategic innovation of digging a trench, and the eventual dispersal of the coalition by a divine wind and internal disputes.

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in the sixth year represents one of the most theologically instructive events of the Medinan period. The Muslims set out for Makkah to perform 'Umrah and were blocked by the Quraysh, leading to negotiations that produced a treaty that appeared disadvantageous on its surface: Muslims who fled Makkah to Madinah would be returned, but Muslims who fled Madinah to Makkah would not be returned; the Muslims would withdraw for that year without performing 'Umrah. Many companions including 'Umar ibn al-Khattab were distressed by the terms, but النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم accepted them, and الله revealed that it was 'a clear victory.'
